coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon dots nanosensor for label-free determination of vitamin B12 based on inner filter effect
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Ding, Longhua
Yang, Hongmei
Ge, Shenguang
Yu, Jinghua
coredata.dc:description: A simple and effective fluorescent assay for the determination of vitamin B12 was developed. In this study, carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method and directly used as a fluorophore in the inner filter effect (IFE). Both of the maximum absorption peak of vitamin B12 and excitation maxima of CDs are located at 360nm, hence, the excited light of CDs can be absorbed by vitamin B12, resulting in the fluorescence reduction of CDs. And the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreases with the increasing concentration of vitamin B12. This IFE-based sensing strategy shows a good linear relationship between the normalized fluorescence intensity and the concentration of vitamin B12 ranging from 0 to 60μM, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.1μM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Furthermore, this proposed approach was successfully applied to vitamin B12 sensing in injections. This IFE sensing platform based on various fluorescent nanomaterials has a high promise for the detection of other biomolecules due to its inherent convenience.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence analysis
Spectral overlap
Inner filter effect
Carbon dots
Vitamin B12

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence Determination of CDs Toward Vitamin B12
Analysis of Vitamin B12 in Injection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (a), the particles size distribution (b), FT-IR (c), XPS (d), C 1s (e) and O 1s (f) spectra of CDs.
(a) UV–vis absorption and (b) fluorescence spectra of CDs under different excitation wavelength.

(A) Normalized fluorescence intensity of CDs alone (a) and in the presence of 5μM vitamin B12. The fluorescence spectra were measured with the excitation at 360nm. (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum of vitamin B12 (a) and fluorescence excitation (b) and emission (c) spectra of CDs. (C) Time-resolved decay of CDs without (a) and with vitamin B12 (b).

(A) Fluorescence responses of CDs in the presence of different concentrations of vitamin B12 (from top to bottom: 0, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 50 and 60μM). (B) The linear relationship between F0/F and the concentration of vitamin B12. F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities at 450nm in the absence and presence of vitamin B12, respectively. The fluorescence spectra were measured with the excitation at 360nm.
Compared with the reported fluorescence methods for detection of vitamin B12.
Effect of interfering substances on fluorescence.
Results for determination of vitamin B12 in pharmaceutical samples.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon dots nanosensor for label-free determination of vitamin B12 based on inner filter effect

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Advanced Interdisciplinary Research, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple and effective fluorescent assay for the determination of vitamin B12 was developed. In this study, carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method and directly used as a fluorophore in the inner filter effect (IFE). Both of the maximum absorption peak of vitamin B12 and excitation maxima of CDs are located at 360nm, hence, the excited light of CDs can be absorbed by vitamin B12, resulting in the fluorescence reduction of CDs. And the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreases with the increasing concentration of vitamin B12. This IFE-based sensing strategy shows a good linear relationship between the normalized fluorescence intensity and the concentration of vitamin B12 ranging from 0 to 60μM, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.1μM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Furthermore, this proposed approach was successfully applied to vitamin B12 sensing in injections. This IFE sensing platform based on various fluorescent nanomaterials has a high promise for the detection of other biomolecules due to its inherent convenience.

ce:section.ce:para: Vitamin B12 contains a cobalt ion within tetrapyrrole ring and is also called cobalamin [1]. It is an essential water soluble nutrient linking to cell development and human growth. Vitamin B12 acts as a coenzyme for human being and participates in multiple metabolic reactions, such as red blood cells formation, nervous system function, DNA synthesis, as well as myelin sheath development [2]. Moreover, the deficiency of vitamin B12 is related with a variety of disorders and human diseases, such as weakness, nausea, fatigue, constipation, pernicious anemia, nerve degeneration and cardiovascular disease [3]. It is well known that vitamin B12 not only cannot be synthesized but also not be stored in the human body because of its hydrosoluble nature. Thus, it must be ingested daily. However, the primary sources of vitamin B12 are mainly from animal products such as fish, meat, liver, eggs, oysters, clams and milk. In order to avoid the nutritional imbalance among the strict vegetarians [4], vitamin B12 is added to many products, including some pharmaceuticals (tables or injections) and energy drinks. However, the daily requirement of vitamin B12 is very low compared to other vitamins [5,6]. In addition, Extra consumption of vitamins may also cause unwanted side effects. Consequently, it is very important to develop a reliable, specific and simple approach for the analysis of vitamin B12.

For decades, a variety methods have been developed to determination vitamin B12, including high performance liquid chromatograph (HPLC), atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), microbiological method, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay, coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS), chemiluminescence, capillary electrophoresis and electrochemical analysis [7]. All these methods have their own limitations. For example, the detection of vitamin B12 with chemiluminescence generally based on the detection of cobalt (II) ions [8]. However, this method is not able to distinguish between cobalt in vitamin B12 and the unbound cobalt [9]. The disadvantages of other methods are containing expensive, time consuming, requirement of expensive instruments and professional operators [10]. Therefore, it is urgent to develop a simple, rapid and high selectivity technique to detection vitamin B12. Recently, the fluorimetric assay for analysis of vitamin B12 has been proposed using organic dyes [11], semiconductor quantum dots [12] and metal nanoclusters [13]. Compared with the organic fluorescent dyes and classic semiconductor quantum dots, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted great interests due to their unique optical and electronic properties, convenient synthesis ways, easy functionalization, low cytotoxicity, excellent chemical and photo stability [14]. These properties enable the development of CDs-based fluorescent sensors for detection of pH [15], metal ions (such as Cu2+, Hg2+ and Ag+, etc.) [16–18], organic molecules (for instance, tetracycline, picric acid and trinitrotoluene) [19–21] and biomolecules (for example, l-cysteine, acetylcholinesterase, alkaline phosphatase, DNA and Micro RNA) [22–27]. The further broadening of CDs application in sensing is interesting and challenging.

During the past years, numerous fluorescence quenching mechanisms have been proposed based on the different photophysical processes, such as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [28], photo induced electron transfer (PET) [29], electronic energy transfer (EET) [30] and inner filter effect (IFE) [31,32]. Among them, the IFE results from the absorption of the excited or emitted light by absorbers in the detection system, wherein the absorption bands of the absorbers overlap with the excitation or emission band of the fluorophores [33]. It is worth mentioning that the IFE based sensors does not require establishing of any covalent linking between the fluorophore and the quencher. Therefore, it is obviously more simple and flexible compared to other fluorescence quenching mechanisms. Over the past years, IFE has been widely applied in optical sensing due to the inherent convenience. For example, Xie group [34] constructed an on-off-on fluorescent carbon dot sensing platform for recognition of chromium (VI) and ascorbic acid based on the IFE. Chen group [35] developed an effective fluorescence sensing method for the determination of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) based on the IFE between graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanosheets and TNP. However, until now, CDs-based sensor via fluorescent inner filter effect for vitamin B12 has never been reported.

Intrigued by these facts, we herein propose a novel type fluorescent assay based on CDs for simple and selective detection of vitamin B12. The fluorescence of CDs is reduced obviously by vitamin B12 because of the perfect overlap between the absorption band of vitamin B12 and the excitation band of CDs. And the fluorescence of CDs gradually decreases with the addition of vitamin B12. The proposed approach also shows its applicability to vitamin B12 in injections.

All chemicals and reagents are analytical grade or higher and used as received without further purification. Citric acid (CA), vitamin C, vitamin B12, vitamin B1 and vitamin B6 were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. l-Cysteine, glucose, urea, uric acid, KCl and ZnCl2 were bought from Aladdin. All aqueous solutions were prepared with high purify water (18MΩ·cm, Milli-Q, Millipore).

CDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal treatment reported previously (Supporting information) [36]. 1.0mL of the stock solution of CDs was mixed with aqueous solution containing different concentrations of vitamin B12 or other disturbers. The final volume of the solution was kept at 3.0mL by adding deionized water. After mixing, all the fluorescence spectra were recorded under excitation at 360nm.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was taken on JEOL 4000 EX microscopy. Infrared spectroscopy (IR) was obtained on a Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum RX (PerkinElmer Spectrometer). Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted on a Focus IS-PEEM X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The fluorescence spectra were measured on a LS-55 spectrophotofluorometer (P.E. USA). The fluorescence lifetime was obtained using a FLS920 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd).

The size, morphology and structure of CDs were confirmed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 1a displays that CDs are well dispersed from each other and the diameter is distributed in range of 2 to 3nm (Fig. 1b), calculating by measuring the average size of 100 single CDs (Image J software). The FT-IR (Fig. 1c) and XPS (Fig. 1d–f) measurements were performed to further explore the chemical composition and the surface functional groups of CDs. The broad absorption bands at 3000–3600cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH and the bands at 2920cm−1 is attributed to the vibration of CH. The bands at ~1720cm−1 and 1633cm−1 are form the stretching of CO and the vibration of CC, confirming that the carboxylic functional group exists on the surface of CDs [15,21,37]. The XPS spectra of the CDs exhibit two peaks at 284 and 532eV, which are attributed to C 1s and O 1s, respectively (Fig. 1d). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 1e) indicates the presence of CC/CC (284.78eV), CO (286.73eV) and CO (288.93eV) groups, respectively [38]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1s (Fig. 1f) exhibits two peaks at 531.48 and 532.73eV, related to CO and COH, respectively [39]. The surface components of the CDs determined by the XPS are in good agreement with FT-IR results.

In order to reveal the spectroscopic properties of the CDs, the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of CDs were studied. As shown in Fig. 2a, CDs present a strong absorption peak at 205nm. Fluorescence spectra of CDs were recorded under excitation wavelengths from 320nm to 400nm. Fig. 2b indicates that the emission band of CDs is depended on the excitation wavelength and the fluorescence maximum is red-shifted with the increase of excitation wavelength. The maximum emission intensity locates at 450nm when CDs are excited at 360nm. And the fluorescent quantum yield of the CDs was calculated to be 5.4% by using quinine sulfate as reference (Supporting information).

In our experiments, we found that the fluorescence of CDs significantly decreased in the presence of vitamin B12 (Fig. 3A). In order to explain this phenomenon, UV–vis absorption of vitamin B12 was recorded. As shown in Fig. 3B-b, vitamin B12 exhibits three absorption peaks at 278, 360, 550nm, respectively [7] and the maximum absorption peak locates at 360nm, overlapping completely with the optimum excitation wavelength of CDs. Therefore, the excited light of CDs can be absorbed by vitamin B12, resulting in the fluorescence reduction of CDs. Both in the FRET and IFE process, there should be a good spectral overlap between the absorption spectrum of a quencher and the excitation or emission spectrum of a fluorophore. In order to ensure the main reason for the fluorescent reduction of CDs, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs was measured. As displayed in Fig. 3C, the lifetime of CDs in the absence and presence of vitamin B12 remained unchanged under an excitation of 370nm, and the fluorescence decay was fitted to yield a lifetime of 4.47ns. These results indicate that the fluorescence reduction of CDs is produced through IFE more than FRET process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence Determination of CDs Toward Vitamin B12
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of pH on the determination of vitamin B12 was investigated. As shown in Fig. S1, the normalized fluorescence responses changed little under various pH solution, indicating that this analytical system has robust pH stability. In order to simplify the procedure, the measurements were conducted by adding various concentration of vitamin B12 to the CDs stock solution directly, as described in experimental section. Fig. 4A displays the vitamin B12 concentration-dependent fluorescence changes of CDs. It is found that the fluorescence intensity at 450nm gradually decrease with the addition of vitamin B12. In addition, there is a good linear relationship between the normalized fluorescence intensity and the concentration of vitamin B12 ranging from 0 to 60μM, as shown in Fig. 4B. The linear regression equation is F0/F=0.96+0.056×c (μM), with a correlation coefficient of 0.997. Furthermore, the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.1μM was calculated based on 3σ/x (σ is the standard deviation of blank signal (S/N=3) and x is the slope of calibration cure). The results in our work are compared with other reported fluorescence methods. From Table 1 , it can be seen that the method we proposed can detection vitamin B12 in a more wide concentration range, and can determinate it in a low detection limit. Moreover, the fluorescence changes of CDs in the presence of vitamin B12 during different times were measured to evaluate its reproducibility. As displayed in Fig. S2, this analytical system was stable within three days, indicating its effectiveness.

In order to evaluate the selectivity, the fluorescence performances of other biological samples including vitamin B6, vitamin B1, l-cysteine, vitamin C, glucose, urea, uric acid K+ and Zn2+ were investigated in the presence of 10μM vitamin B12. The results are shown in Table 2 . It indicates that most of the biological samples and metal ions have little influence on vitamin B12. And the UV–vis absorption spectra of these interference substances were measured. As shown in Fig. S3, the maximum absorption wavelength of these substances were all shorter than 330nm and had no spectra overlay with CDs. Hence, the proposed method displays an excellent selectivity in the determination of vitamin B12.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Vitamin B12 in Injection
ce:section.ce:para: In order to certify the availability of this proposed method, it was applied for the determination of vitamin B12 in injection. 5.0mL of vitamin B12 injection (label value: 0.5mg·mL−1) was mixed with distilled water to a final volume of 25mL to get 0.1mg·mL−1 solution. And the concentrations required in the following experiments were adjusted with distilled water on the basis of the stock solution. It was diluted to four different concentrations (denoted as injection 1, 2, 3 and 4) before measurement. And all the samples were analyzed for three times at least under the same conditions. The analytical results are shown in Table 3 and the quantity of vitamin B12 is in good agreement with the amounts claimed by the manufactures. These results indicate that the proposed approach is efficient and reliable.

In summary, a fluorescent vitamin B12 analytical system has been constructed based on the competitive absorption of CDs and vitamin B12. This fluorescence assay method enables the sensing of vitamin B12 in the range of 0–60μM with a detection limit of 0.1μM. Compared with the previous methods for vitamin B12 detection, this approach can be conducted in a convenient way, and it also exhibits a good sensitivity and selectivity. Moreover, this proposed nanosensor exhibits satisfactory results for the determination of vitamin B12 in injections.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the Shandong Distinguished Middle-Aged and Young Scientist Encourage and Reward Foundation (ZR2016BB29), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21575051).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2017.12.015.
